key_insight: An optimization-based phase-field calibration can embed sharp-interface solute trapping and solute-drag response functions into diffuse-interface simulations of rapid alloy solidification.
engineering_relevance: This enables more quantitative prediction and control of microsegregation and solidification morphology in rapid casting and additive-manufacturing-like conditions.
method: Treat interfacial diffusivity interpolation as a tunable degree of freedom.
Fit 1D steady-state phase-field solutions to continuous-growth-model targets for velocity-dependent trapping and drag-modified liquidus kinetics.
Apply the calibrated model in 2D directional-solidification simulations and extend it to multicomponent dilute alloys.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that the calibrated model reproduces prescribed response functions in dilute Al-Cu and that 2D simulations capture morphology shifts as solute drag increases.
watch_item: The approach depends on accurate target response functions and on 1D calibration assumptions, so transferability to complex geometries or strongly non-dilute systems may require additional validation.
paper_title: Response-function-optimized phase field modeling of solute trapping and solute drag in rapid alloy solidification
